  4. Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages

Lab 2 – Seasonality, Decomposition & Moving Averages

STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis

Author

Study Guide

Published

March 31, 2026

📝 How to use this guide: Read each section carefully before running the code. Every code chunk has a plain-English explanation above it. The 🧠 icons mark key interpretation points your lecturer expects you to discuss in the exam.

1 What This Lab Is About

In Lab 2 we build on what we learned in Lab 1. The big ideas are:

  • Every time series can be broken into components (trend, seasonality, random noise).
  • We can model those components in two ways: additive or multiplicative.
  • We use a tool called decomposition to pull these components apart and look at each one.
  • We use moving averages to smooth out the noise and see the trend more clearly.

Think of it like this: imagine you own an ice-cream shop. Sales go up every summer and down every winter (that’s seasonality). But over years, sales are generally rising because you opened more shops (that’s the trend). And some weeks are weirdly good or bad for no reason (that’s the random part). Decomposition helps you separate these three stories.

2 Background: The Two Decomposition Models

2.1 The Core Formula

Every observation \(y_t\) in a time series is made up of three pieces:

\[y_t = f(S_t, T_t, R_t)\]

Where:

Symbol Name Meaning
\(y_t\) Observation The actual value you recorded at time \(t\)
\(S_t\) Seasonal component The repeating pattern (e.g., higher sales every December)
\(T_t\) Trend-cycle component The long-run direction (going up? going down? flat?)
\(R_t\) Random (Remainder) component Everything left over — pure noise

2.2 Additive Model

\[y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t\]

Use when: The seasonal swings stay roughly the same size over time (called homogeneous seasonality).

🧠 How to spot it: Look at the plot. If the peaks and troughs are about the same height throughout the whole series → use additive.

Seasonal adjustment (additive):

\[\text{Seasonally Adjusted} = y_t - S_t = T_t + R_t\]

You literally subtract the seasonal part away to see just the trend + noise.

2.3 Multiplicative Model

\[y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t\]

Use when: The seasonal swings grow bigger as the trend goes up (called increasing seasonality). Think of airline passengers — summer peaks get bigger every year as more people fly.

🧠 How to spot it: Look at the plot. If the peaks and troughs are getting larger over time → use multiplicative.

Seasonal adjustment (multiplicative):

\[\text{Seasonally Adjusted} = \frac{y_t}{S_t} = T_t \times R_t\]

You divide out the seasonal part.

2.4 The Trick: Turning Multiplicative Into Additive

If you take the logarithm of a multiplicative model, it becomes additive:

\[\log(y_t) = \log(S_t) + \log(T_t) + \log(R_t)\]

This is called a Box-Cox transformation. It’s very useful because additive models are simpler to work with!

3 Part 1 – Manual Decomposition from a Table

3.1 The Data

The lab gives us this quarterly data (3 years, 4 quarters each):

Year Quarter Value X
1 1 5.0
1 2 6.0
1 3 6.5
1 4 6.3
2 1 7.5
2 2 8.3
2 3 8.4
2 4 7.8
3 1 8.7
3 2 9.4
3 3 9.7
3 4 9.6

3.2 Step 1: Enter the Data and Plot It

# ── Step 1: Create the time series ──────────────────────────────────────────
# We have quarterly data (frequency = 4), starting in Year 1, Quarter 1

x <- c(5.0, 6.0, 6.5, 6.3,   # Year 1
        7.5, 8.3, 8.4, 7.8,   # Year 2
        8.7, 9.4, 9.7, 9.6)   # Year 3

x_ts <- ts(x, frequency = 4, start = c(1, 1))

# ── Step 2: Plot it ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
plot(x_ts,
     main = "Quarterly Time Series X",
     ylab = "Value", xlab = "Year",
     col  = "steelblue", lwd = 2)

🧠 What to look for when you plot:

  • Is there an upward or downward trend? → Yes, values go from ~5 up to ~9.7
  • Is there a repeating seasonal pattern? → Q3 tends to be a peak, Q1 tends to be a trough
  • Are the seasonal swings (the up-down wiggles) staying the same size, or growing? → They look roughly constant in size → this suggests additive model

3.3 Step 2: Decompose Using R’s decompose() Function

# ── Additive decomposition (default) ────────────────────────────────────────
decomp_add <- decompose(x_ts, type = "additive")
plot(decomp_add)
# ── Multiplicative decomposition ─────────────────────────────────────────────
decomp_mult <- decompose(x_ts, type = "multiplicative")
plot(decomp_mult)

🧠 How to read the decomposition plots:

The decompose() function produces 4 panels stacked on top of each other:

  1. observed — the original raw data (what you started with)
  2. trend — the smooth long-run direction (note: there will be missing values at the start and end — this is normal! It happens because the moving average needs data on both sides of each point)
  3. seasonal — the repeating pattern. For quarterly data you should see the same 4-quarter wave repeating every year.
  4. random — the leftover noise after removing trend and seasonality.

⚠️ Missing values in trend: With quarterly data (frequency = 4), you’ll lose 4/2 = 2 observations at the beginning and 2 at the end of the trend line. This is expected.

3.4 Step 3: Which Model Is Better? — Look at the Residuals (Random Component)

# Extract and plot just the random component for both models
par(mfrow = c(2, 1))  # Put 2 plots on top of each other

plot(decomp_add$random,
     main = "Residuals – Additive Model",
     col = "darkblue", ylab = "Residual")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)

plot(decomp_mult$random,
     main = "Residuals – Multiplicative Model",
     col = "darkgreen", ylab = "Residual")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))  # Reset layout

🧠 The golden rule for residuals: A good model leaves behind residuals that look like pure random noise — no patterns, no trends, just random up-and-down around zero. We also want the spread (variance) to be roughly constant over time.

  • If you can still see a wave pattern in the residuals → the model hasn’t captured seasonality properly
  • If the spread of residuals gets bigger over time → try the other model

3.5 Step 4: Seasonal Adjustment (Manually Remove Seasonality)

# ── Additive: subtract the seasonal component ────────────────────────────────
seasonal_adj_add <- x_ts - decomp_add$seasonal
plot(seasonal_adj_add,
     main = "Seasonally Adjusted Series (Additive)",
     col = "darkorange", lwd = 2, ylab = "Adjusted Value")
# ── Multiplicative: divide by the seasonal component ─────────────────────────
seasonal_adj_mult <- x_ts / decomp_mult$seasonal
plot(seasonal_adj_mult,
     main = "Seasonally Adjusted Series (Multiplicative)",
     col = "purple", lwd = 2, ylab = "Adjusted Value")

🧠 What does seasonal adjustment give us? It shows us \(T_t + R_t\) (or \(T_t \times R_t\) for multiplicative). The series is not smooth — it still has the random noise. But the regular seasonal peaks and troughs are gone. This lets you see the underlying trend more clearly, and also spot unusual events (like a sudden spike that isn’t seasonal).

4 Part 2 – Remove Seasonality from the Souvenirs Dataset

The souvenirs dataset is built into R’s forecast package. It records monthly souvenir sales at a beach resort.

library(forecast)   # Contains the souvenirs dataset and many useful functions

# Load and plot the data
data(souvenirs)
plot(souvenirs,
     main = "Monthly Souvenir Sales",
     ylab = "Sales", xlab = "Year",
     col = "steelblue", lwd = 2)

🧠 Look at the souvenirs plot carefully:

  • Is there a trend? → Yes, strong upward trend
  • Is there seasonality? → Yes, big spike every December (Christmas sales)
  • Are the seasonal spikes growing as time goes on? → Yes! The December spikes get much bigger as sales grow overall

This growing seasonality is the key signal → multiplicative model is more appropriate here.

# Decompose – try both and compare
decomp_souv_add  <- decompose(souvenirs, type = "additive")
decomp_souv_mult <- decompose(souvenirs, type = "multiplicative")

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(decomp_souv_add,  main = "Additive")
plot(decomp_souv_mult, main = "Multiplicative")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
# ── Manually remove seasonality (multiplicative) ─────────────────────────────
souv_adjusted <- souvenirs / decomp_souv_mult$seasonal

plot(souvenirs, col = "lightblue", lwd = 1,
     main = "Souvenirs: Original vs Seasonally Adjusted",
     ylab = "Sales")
lines(souv_adjusted, col = "red", lwd = 2)
legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Original", "Seasonally Adjusted"),
       col    = c("lightblue", "red"),
       lwd    = c(1, 2))

🧠 Interpretation: After removing the seasonal component, the red line should be much smoother — no more huge December spikes. What remains is the trend and random noise. You can now clearly see that sales are growing over the years.

5 Part 3 – Shampoo Sales Dataset

5.1 Load the Data

# Read the shampoo data directly from the web
shampoo_raw <- read.csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jbrownlee/Datasets/master/shampoo.csv",
  header = TRUE
)

# Inspect what we got
head(shampoo_raw)
str(shampoo_raw)
# The data is monthly – 3 years, 36 months
# We only need the Sales column (column 2)
shampoo_ts <- ts(shampoo_raw[, 2],
                 frequency = 12,   # 12 months per year
                 start = c(1, 1))  # Start at Year 1, Month 1

plot(shampoo_ts,
     main = "Monthly Shampoo Sales",
     ylab = "Sales", xlab = "Year",
     col  = "steelblue", lwd = 2)

5.2 Additive or Multiplicative? – The Discussion

🧠 This is the key exam-style question. Here’s how to think through it:

Step 1 – Look at the trend: Is there a clear upward or downward movement?
→ Yes, shampoo sales increase strongly over the 3 years.

Step 2 – Look at the seasonal swings: Do the peaks/troughs stay the same size, or do they grow along with the trend?
→ As sales increase, the swings tend to grow proportionally → this suggests multiplicative.

Step 3 – Confirm with decomposition: After decomposing, look at the residuals. The model with more random-looking residuals (no patterns, constant variance) is the better one.

# Decompose both ways
decomp_sh_add  <- decompose(shampoo_ts, type = "additive")
decomp_sh_mult <- decompose(shampoo_ts, type = "multiplicative")

par(mfrow = c(2, 1))
plot(decomp_sh_add,  main = "Shampoo – Additive Decomposition")
plot(decomp_sh_mult, main = "Shampoo – Multiplicative Decomposition")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
# Compare the residuals side by side
par(mfrow = c(2, 1))
plot(decomp_sh_add$random,
     main = "Residuals – Additive", col = "darkblue", ylab = "")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)

plot(decomp_sh_mult$random,
     main = "Residuals – Multiplicative", col = "darkgreen", ylab = "")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

5.3 Which Components Should Be in the Model?

🧠 Exam-style answer template:

For the shampoo data, the model should include:

  • ✅ Trend — there is a clear and strong upward trend over the 3 years
  • ✅ Seasonality — there are repeating monthly patterns (some months sell more than others)
  • ✅ Random component — there is always some unexplained noise

Given the growing seasonal variation, the multiplicative model is the most appropriate:

\[y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t\]

6 Part 4 – Simple Moving Average

6.1 What Is a Moving Average? (ELI5 Version)

Imagine you get a test score every week. One bad week (you were sick) makes the graph spike downward. A moving average smooths this out by saying: “instead of showing your score this week, show the average of this week and the weeks around it.” The spikes disappear and you can see the real trend.

Formally, for a moving average of order \(m\) (where \(m\) is odd):

\[MA_t = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=-(m-1)/2}^{(m-1)/2} y_{t+j}\]

For a 5-point moving average (m = 5), each smoothed value is the average of 2 values before, the current value, and 2 values after.

6.2 Key Formula

\[F_t = MA_p = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} D_i}{p}\]

Where: - \(F_t\) = forecast/smoothed value at time \(t\) - \(p\) = the order (number of periods to average) - \(D_i\) = the observed data values

6.3 Births Dataset – How m Affects the Smoothing

# Install fpp3 if you haven't already (only run once!)
# install.packages("fpp3")
library(fpp3)

# ── Download the NY births data ──────────────────────────────────────────────
births <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/data/nybirths.dat")

# ── Convert to time series: monthly, starting January 1946 ──────────────────
births <- ts(births, frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1))

# ── Convert to a tsibble (modern time series format used by fpp3) ─────────────
births_tsibble <- as_tsibble(births)
names(births_tsibble) <- c("year_month", "births_per_month")

head(births_tsibble)
# ── Plot the raw data first ────────────────────────────────────────────────
plot(births,
     main = "Monthly Births in New York City (1946–1959)",
     ylab = "Births per Month",
     xlab = "Year",
     col  = "steelblue", lwd = 1)

🧠 What to see here: - Clear trend (births gradually change over the years) - Clear seasonality (more births in summer months than winter) - Some random noise from month to month

6.4 Calculating Moving Averages with Different Orders of m

# ── Using the slider package (part of fpp3) ──────────────────────────────────
# before = how many values before current point to include
# after  = how many values after current point to include
# complete = TRUE means: only calculate if you have a full window (no partial edges)

library(slider)

# 5-month moving average (2 before + current + 2 after)
MA_5  <- slider::slide_mean(births_tsibble$births_per_month,
                             before = 2, after = 2, complete = TRUE)

# 11-month moving average (5 before + current + 5 after)
MA_11 <- slider::slide_mean(births_tsibble$births_per_month,
                             before = 5, after = 5, complete = TRUE)

# 3-month moving average (1 before + current + 1 after)
MA_3  <- slider::slide_mean(births_tsibble$births_per_month,
                             before = 1, after = 1, complete = TRUE)
# ── Plot all three together to see how m affects smoothing ──────────────────
plot(births,
     main = "NY Births – Moving Averages with Different Orders of m",
     ylab = "Births per Month", xlab = "Year",
     col  = "lightgray", lwd = 1)

lines(ts(MA_3,  frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1)),
      col = "blue",   lwd = 2)
lines(ts(MA_5,  frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1)),
      col = "red",    lwd = 2)
lines(ts(MA_11, frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1)),
      col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2)

legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Original", "MA(3)", "MA(5)", "MA(11)"),
       col    = c("lightgray", "blue", "red", "darkgreen"),
       lwd    = c(1, 2, 2, 2))

🧠 The effect of m – key interpretation:

m is… Effect
Small (e.g. 3) Less smoothing. The line follows the data closely. You still see the seasonal wiggles.
Large (e.g. 11 or 12) More smoothing. The seasonal pattern gets averaged out. You see only the trend.
Equal to periodicity (e.g. 12 for monthly) Removes the seasonal component completely → shows pure trend

💡 Rule of thumb: To extract the trend from monthly data (frequency = 12), use m = 12 (or a centred 2×12 moving average for even orders).

6.5 Alternative Implementations of Moving Average

# ── Using the forecast package ────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(forecast)

# ma() from forecast package — simple to use
births_ma_forecast <- ma(births, order = 5)

# ── Using the TTR package ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(TTR)

# SMA() = Simple Moving Average
births_ma_ttr <- SMA(births, n = 5)

# ── Using the zoo package ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(zoo)

# rollmean() with a window of 5, align = "center"
births_ma_zoo <- rollmean(births, k = 5, fill = NA, align = "center")
# ── Compare all implementations ──────────────────────────────────────────────
plot(births, col = "lightgray", lwd = 1,
     main = "Comparing MA Implementations (m=5)",
     ylab = "Births")
lines(births_ma_forecast, col = "red",  lwd = 2, lty = 1)
lines(births_ma_ttr,      col = "blue", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
lines(births_ma_zoo,      col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2, lty = 3)

legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Original", "forecast::ma()", "TTR::SMA()", "zoo::rollmean()"),
       col    = c("lightgray", "red", "blue", "darkgreen"),
       lwd    = c(1, 2, 2, 2),
       lty    = c(1, 1, 2, 3))

🧠 They should all give the same result! Slight differences may exist in how they handle the edges (missing values at the beginning and end), but the central values should match. This is a good way to double-check your calculations.

7 Key Equations Summary

Concept Formula
Additive decomposition \(y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t\)
Multiplicative decomposition \(y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t\)
Additive seasonal adjustment \(y_t - S_t = T_t + R_t\)
Multiplicative seasonal adjustment \(y_t / S_t = T_t \times R_t\)
Log transform (multiplicative → additive) \(\log(y_t) = \log S_t + \log T_t + \log R_t\)
Simple Moving Average \(MA_t = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=-(m-1)/2}^{(m-1)/2} y_{t+j}\)
Missing values from MA \(\frac{\text{frequency}}{2}\) at each end

8 Summary Bullet Points

  • A time series has up to three components: Trend (\(T_t\)), Seasonality (\(S_t\)), and Random (\(R_t\)).
  • Use the additive model (\(y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t\)) when seasonal variation is constant over time.
  • Use the multiplicative model (\(y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t\)) when seasonal variation grows with the level of the series.
  • The decompose() function in R splits a time series into its components automatically. Always plot the result.
  • To choose between additive/multiplicative: look at the residuals — they should look like random noise (no pattern, constant variance).
  • Seasonal adjustment removes \(S_t\) so you can see the trend and noise more clearly. For additive: subtract \(S_t\). For multiplicative: divide by \(S_t\).
  • A Moving Average of order m smooths the series. The larger \(m\) is, the smoother the result. Using \(m =\) periodicity removes seasonality entirely.
  • There will always be missing values at the edges of a moving average: \(\frac{m-1}{2}\) at each end for odd \(m\), or \(\frac{\text{frequency}}{2}\) for seasonal MA.
  • Multiple R packages (forecast, TTR, zoo, slider) can calculate moving averages and should give the same core result.

9 Notes & Annotations Space

✏️ Use this space to add your own notes during revision or after class discussion.

Additional notes on additive vs multiplicative decision:

Additional notes on interpreting residuals:

Questions to ask the lecturer:

This document was created as a study guide for Lab 2. Run all code chunks in RStudio for interactive exploration.

STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis
Time Series: Classical Models Lab

> 📝 **How to use this guide:** Read each section carefully *before* running the code.
> Every code chunk has a plain-English explanation above it. The 🧠 icons mark
> key interpretation points your lecturer expects you to discuss in the exam.

---

# What This Lab Is About

In Lab 2 we build on what we learned in Lab 1. The big ideas are:

- Every time series can be broken into **components** (trend, seasonality, random noise).
- We can model those components in two ways: **additive** or **multiplicative**.
- We use a tool called **decomposition** to pull these components apart and look at each one.
- We use **moving averages** to smooth out the noise and see the trend more clearly.

Think of it like this: imagine you own an ice-cream shop. Sales go up every summer and down every winter (that's **seasonality**). But over years, sales are generally rising because you opened more shops (that's the **trend**). And some weeks are weirdly good or bad for no reason (that's the **random** part). Decomposition helps you separate these three stories.

---

# Background: The Two Decomposition Models

## The Core Formula

Every observation $y_t$ in a time series is made up of three pieces:

$$y_t = f(S_t, T_t, R_t)$$

Where:

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|--------|------|---------|
| $y_t$ | Observation | The actual value you recorded at time $t$ |
| $S_t$ | Seasonal component | The repeating pattern (e.g., higher sales every December) |
| $T_t$ | Trend-cycle component | The long-run direction (going up? going down? flat?) |
| $R_t$ | Random (Remainder) component | Everything left over — pure noise |

## Additive Model

$$y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t$$

**Use when:** The seasonal swings stay roughly the **same size** over time (called *homogeneous seasonality*).

🧠 **How to spot it:** Look at the plot. If the peaks and troughs are about the same height throughout the whole series → use **additive**.

**Seasonal adjustment (additive):**

$$\text{Seasonally Adjusted} = y_t - S_t = T_t + R_t$$

> You literally subtract the seasonal part away to see just the trend + noise.

## Multiplicative Model

$$y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t$$

**Use when:** The seasonal swings **grow bigger** as the trend goes up (called *increasing seasonality*). Think of airline passengers — summer peaks get bigger every year as more people fly.

🧠 **How to spot it:** Look at the plot. If the peaks and troughs are getting *larger* over time → use **multiplicative**.

**Seasonal adjustment (multiplicative):**

$$\text{Seasonally Adjusted} = \frac{y_t}{S_t} = T_t \times R_t$$

> You divide out the seasonal part.

## The Trick: Turning Multiplicative Into Additive

If you take the **logarithm** of a multiplicative model, it becomes additive:

$$\log(y_t) = \log(S_t) + \log(T_t) + \log(R_t)$$

This is called a **Box-Cox transformation**. It's very useful because additive models are simpler to work with!

---

# Part 1 – Manual Decomposition from a Table

## The Data

The lab gives us this quarterly data (3 years, 4 quarters each):

| Year | Quarter | Value X |
|------|---------|---------|
| 1    | 1       | 5.0     |
| 1    | 2       | 6.0     |
| 1    | 3       | 6.5     |
| 1    | 4       | 6.3     |
| 2    | 1       | 7.5     |
| 2    | 2       | 8.3     |
| 2    | 3       | 8.4     |
| 2    | 4       | 7.8     |
| 3    | 1       | 8.7     |
| 3    | 2       | 9.4     |
| 3    | 3       | 9.7     |
| 3    | 4       | 9.6     |

## Step 1: Enter the Data and Plot It

```{r}
# ── Step 1: Create the time series ──────────────────────────────────────────
# We have quarterly data (frequency = 4), starting in Year 1, Quarter 1

x <- c(5.0, 6.0, 6.5, 6.3,   # Year 1
        7.5, 8.3, 8.4, 7.8,   # Year 2
        8.7, 9.4, 9.7, 9.6)   # Year 3

x_ts <- ts(x, frequency = 4, start = c(1, 1))

# ── Step 2: Plot it ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
plot(x_ts,
     main = "Quarterly Time Series X",
     ylab = "Value", xlab = "Year",
     col  = "steelblue", lwd = 2)
```

🧠 **What to look for when you plot:**

- Is there an **upward or downward trend**? → Yes, values go from ~5 up to ~9.7
- Is there a **repeating seasonal pattern**? → Q3 tends to be a peak, Q1 tends to be a trough
- Are the seasonal swings (the up-down wiggles) staying the same size, or growing? → They look roughly *constant* in size → this suggests **additive** model

## Step 2: Decompose Using R's `decompose()` Function

```{r}
# ── Additive decomposition (default) ────────────────────────────────────────
decomp_add <- decompose(x_ts, type = "additive")
plot(decomp_add)
```

```{r}
# ── Multiplicative decomposition ─────────────────────────────────────────────
decomp_mult <- decompose(x_ts, type = "multiplicative")
plot(decomp_mult)
```

🧠 **How to read the decomposition plots:**

The `decompose()` function produces 4 panels stacked on top of each other:

1. **observed** — the original raw data (what you started with)
2. **trend** — the smooth long-run direction (note: there will be **missing values** at the start and end — this is normal! It happens because the moving average needs data on both sides of each point)
3. **seasonal** — the repeating pattern. For quarterly data you should see the same 4-quarter wave repeating every year.
4. **random** — the leftover noise after removing trend and seasonality.

> ⚠️ **Missing values in trend:** With quarterly data (frequency = 4), you'll lose `4/2 = 2` observations at the beginning and 2 at the end of the trend line. This is expected.

## Step 3: Which Model Is Better? — Look at the Residuals (Random Component)

```{r}
# Extract and plot just the random component for both models
par(mfrow = c(2, 1))  # Put 2 plots on top of each other

plot(decomp_add$random,
     main = "Residuals – Additive Model",
     col = "darkblue", ylab = "Residual")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)

plot(decomp_mult$random,
     main = "Residuals – Multiplicative Model",
     col = "darkgreen", ylab = "Residual")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))  # Reset layout
```

🧠 **The golden rule for residuals:** A *good* model leaves behind residuals that look like **pure random noise** — no patterns, no trends, just random up-and-down around zero. We also want the spread (variance) to be roughly constant over time.

- If you can still see a wave pattern in the residuals → the model hasn't captured seasonality properly
- If the spread of residuals gets bigger over time → try the other model

## Step 4: Seasonal Adjustment (Manually Remove Seasonality)

```{r}
# ── Additive: subtract the seasonal component ────────────────────────────────
seasonal_adj_add <- x_ts - decomp_add$seasonal
plot(seasonal_adj_add,
     main = "Seasonally Adjusted Series (Additive)",
     col = "darkorange", lwd = 2, ylab = "Adjusted Value")
```

```{r}
# ── Multiplicative: divide by the seasonal component ─────────────────────────
seasonal_adj_mult <- x_ts / decomp_mult$seasonal
plot(seasonal_adj_mult,
     main = "Seasonally Adjusted Series (Multiplicative)",
     col = "purple", lwd = 2, ylab = "Adjusted Value")
```

🧠 **What does seasonal adjustment give us?** It shows us $T_t + R_t$ (or $T_t \times R_t$ for multiplicative). The series is *not* smooth — it still has the random noise. But the regular seasonal peaks and troughs are gone. This lets you see the underlying trend more clearly, and also spot unusual events (like a sudden spike that isn't seasonal).

---

# Part 2 – Remove Seasonality from the Souvenirs Dataset

The `souvenirs` dataset is built into R's `forecast` package. It records monthly souvenir sales at a beach resort.

```{r}
library(forecast)   # Contains the souvenirs dataset and many useful functions

# Load and plot the data
data(souvenirs)
plot(souvenirs,
     main = "Monthly Souvenir Sales",
     ylab = "Sales", xlab = "Year",
     col = "steelblue", lwd = 2)
```

🧠 **Look at the souvenirs plot carefully:**

- Is there a trend? → Yes, strong upward trend
- Is there seasonality? → Yes, big spike every December (Christmas sales)
- Are the seasonal spikes *growing* as time goes on? → **Yes!** The December spikes get much bigger as sales grow overall

This growing seasonality is the key signal → **multiplicative model** is more appropriate here.

```{r}
# Decompose – try both and compare
decomp_souv_add  <- decompose(souvenirs, type = "additive")
decomp_souv_mult <- decompose(souvenirs, type = "multiplicative")

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(decomp_souv_add,  main = "Additive")
plot(decomp_souv_mult, main = "Multiplicative")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

```{r}
# ── Manually remove seasonality (multiplicative) ─────────────────────────────
souv_adjusted <- souvenirs / decomp_souv_mult$seasonal

plot(souvenirs, col = "lightblue", lwd = 1,
     main = "Souvenirs: Original vs Seasonally Adjusted",
     ylab = "Sales")
lines(souv_adjusted, col = "red", lwd = 2)
legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Original", "Seasonally Adjusted"),
       col    = c("lightblue", "red"),
       lwd    = c(1, 2))
```

🧠 **Interpretation:** After removing the seasonal component, the red line should be much smoother — no more huge December spikes. What remains is the trend and random noise. You can now clearly see that sales are growing over the years.

---

# Part 3 – Shampoo Sales Dataset

## Load the Data

```{r}
# Read the shampoo data directly from the web
shampoo_raw <- read.csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jbrownlee/Datasets/master/shampoo.csv",
  header = TRUE
)

# Inspect what we got
head(shampoo_raw)
str(shampoo_raw)
```

```{r}
# The data is monthly – 3 years, 36 months
# We only need the Sales column (column 2)
shampoo_ts <- ts(shampoo_raw[, 2],
                 frequency = 12,   # 12 months per year
                 start = c(1, 1))  # Start at Year 1, Month 1

plot(shampoo_ts,
     main = "Monthly Shampoo Sales",
     ylab = "Sales", xlab = "Year",
     col  = "steelblue", lwd = 2)
```

## Additive or Multiplicative? – The Discussion

🧠 **This is the key exam-style question.** Here's how to think through it:

**Step 1 – Look at the trend:** Is there a clear upward or downward movement?  
→ Yes, shampoo sales increase strongly over the 3 years.

**Step 2 – Look at the seasonal swings:** Do the peaks/troughs stay the same size, or do they grow along with the trend?  
→ As sales increase, the swings tend to grow proportionally → this suggests **multiplicative**.

**Step 3 – Confirm with decomposition:** After decomposing, look at the residuals. The model with more random-looking residuals (no patterns, constant variance) is the better one.

```{r}
# Decompose both ways
decomp_sh_add  <- decompose(shampoo_ts, type = "additive")
decomp_sh_mult <- decompose(shampoo_ts, type = "multiplicative")

par(mfrow = c(2, 1))
plot(decomp_sh_add,  main = "Shampoo – Additive Decomposition")
plot(decomp_sh_mult, main = "Shampoo – Multiplicative Decomposition")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

```{r}
# Compare the residuals side by side
par(mfrow = c(2, 1))
plot(decomp_sh_add$random,
     main = "Residuals – Additive", col = "darkblue", ylab = "")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)

plot(decomp_sh_mult$random,
     main = "Residuals – Multiplicative", col = "darkgreen", ylab = "")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

## Which Components Should Be in the Model?

🧠 **Exam-style answer template:**

For the shampoo data, the model should include:

- ✅ **Trend** — there is a clear and strong upward trend over the 3 years
- ✅ **Seasonality** — there are repeating monthly patterns (some months sell more than others)
- ✅ **Random component** — there is always some unexplained noise

Given the growing seasonal variation, the **multiplicative model** is the most appropriate:

$$y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t$$

---

# Part 4 – Simple Moving Average

## What Is a Moving Average? (ELI5 Version)

Imagine you get a test score every week. One bad week (you were sick) makes the graph spike downward. A **moving average** smooths this out by saying: "instead of showing your score *this* week, show the average of this week and the weeks around it." The spikes disappear and you can see the real trend.

Formally, for a moving average of order $m$ (where $m$ is odd):

$$MA_t = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=-(m-1)/2}^{(m-1)/2} y_{t+j}$$

For a **5-point moving average** (m = 5), each smoothed value is the average of 2 values before, the current value, and 2 values after.

## Key Formula

$$F_t = MA_p = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} D_i}{p}$$

Where:
- $F_t$ = forecast/smoothed value at time $t$
- $p$ = the order (number of periods to average)
- $D_i$ = the observed data values

## Births Dataset – How m Affects the Smoothing

```{r}
# Install fpp3 if you haven't already (only run once!)
# install.packages("fpp3")
library(fpp3)

# ── Download the NY births data ──────────────────────────────────────────────
births <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/data/nybirths.dat")

# ── Convert to time series: monthly, starting January 1946 ──────────────────
births <- ts(births, frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1))

# ── Convert to a tsibble (modern time series format used by fpp3) ─────────────
births_tsibble <- as_tsibble(births)
names(births_tsibble) <- c("year_month", "births_per_month")

head(births_tsibble)
```

```{r}
# ── Plot the raw data first ────────────────────────────────────────────────
plot(births,
     main = "Monthly Births in New York City (1946–1959)",
     ylab = "Births per Month",
     xlab = "Year",
     col  = "steelblue", lwd = 1)
```

🧠 **What to see here:**
- Clear **trend** (births gradually change over the years)
- Clear **seasonality** (more births in summer months than winter)
- Some **random** noise from month to month

## Calculating Moving Averages with Different Orders of m

```{r}
# ── Using the slider package (part of fpp3) ──────────────────────────────────
# before = how many values before current point to include
# after  = how many values after current point to include
# complete = TRUE means: only calculate if you have a full window (no partial edges)

library(slider)

# 5-month moving average (2 before + current + 2 after)
MA_5  <- slider::slide_mean(births_tsibble$births_per_month,
                             before = 2, after = 2, complete = TRUE)

# 11-month moving average (5 before + current + 5 after)
MA_11 <- slider::slide_mean(births_tsibble$births_per_month,
                             before = 5, after = 5, complete = TRUE)

# 3-month moving average (1 before + current + 1 after)
MA_3  <- slider::slide_mean(births_tsibble$births_per_month,
                             before = 1, after = 1, complete = TRUE)
```

```{r}
# ── Plot all three together to see how m affects smoothing ──────────────────
plot(births,
     main = "NY Births – Moving Averages with Different Orders of m",
     ylab = "Births per Month", xlab = "Year",
     col  = "lightgray", lwd = 1)

lines(ts(MA_3,  frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1)),
      col = "blue",   lwd = 2)
lines(ts(MA_5,  frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1)),
      col = "red",    lwd = 2)
lines(ts(MA_11, frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1)),
      col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2)

legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Original", "MA(3)", "MA(5)", "MA(11)"),
       col    = c("lightgray", "blue", "red", "darkgreen"),
       lwd    = c(1, 2, 2, 2))
```

🧠 **The effect of m – key interpretation:**

| m is... | Effect |
|---------|--------|
| **Small (e.g. 3)** | Less smoothing. The line follows the data closely. You still see the seasonal wiggles. |
| **Large (e.g. 11 or 12)** | More smoothing. The seasonal pattern gets averaged out. You see only the **trend**. |
| **Equal to periodicity (e.g. 12 for monthly)** | Removes the seasonal component completely → shows pure trend |

> 💡 **Rule of thumb:** To extract the trend from monthly data (frequency = 12), use m = 12 (or a centred 2×12 moving average for even orders).

## Alternative Implementations of Moving Average

```{r}
# ── Using the forecast package ────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(forecast)

# ma() from forecast package — simple to use
births_ma_forecast <- ma(births, order = 5)

# ── Using the TTR package ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(TTR)

# SMA() = Simple Moving Average
births_ma_ttr <- SMA(births, n = 5)

# ── Using the zoo package ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(zoo)

# rollmean() with a window of 5, align = "center"
births_ma_zoo <- rollmean(births, k = 5, fill = NA, align = "center")
```

```{r}
# ── Compare all implementations ──────────────────────────────────────────────
plot(births, col = "lightgray", lwd = 1,
     main = "Comparing MA Implementations (m=5)",
     ylab = "Births")
lines(births_ma_forecast, col = "red",  lwd = 2, lty = 1)
lines(births_ma_ttr,      col = "blue", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
lines(births_ma_zoo,      col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2, lty = 3)

legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Original", "forecast::ma()", "TTR::SMA()", "zoo::rollmean()"),
       col    = c("lightgray", "red", "blue", "darkgreen"),
       lwd    = c(1, 2, 2, 2),
       lty    = c(1, 1, 2, 3))
```

🧠 **They should all give the same result!** Slight differences may exist in how they handle the edges (missing values at the beginning and end), but the central values should match. This is a good way to double-check your calculations.

---

# Key Equations Summary

| Concept | Formula |
|---------|---------|
| Additive decomposition | $y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t$ |
| Multiplicative decomposition | $y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t$ |
| Additive seasonal adjustment | $y_t - S_t = T_t + R_t$ |
| Multiplicative seasonal adjustment | $y_t / S_t = T_t \times R_t$ |
| Log transform (multiplicative → additive) | $\log(y_t) = \log S_t + \log T_t + \log R_t$ |
| Simple Moving Average | $MA_t = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=-(m-1)/2}^{(m-1)/2} y_{t+j}$ |
| Missing values from MA | $\frac{\text{frequency}}{2}$ at each end |

---

# Summary Bullet Points

- A **time series** has up to three components: **Trend** ($T_t$), **Seasonality** ($S_t$), and **Random** ($R_t$).
- Use the **additive model** ($y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t$) when seasonal variation is **constant** over time.
- Use the **multiplicative model** ($y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t$) when seasonal variation **grows** with the level of the series.
- The **`decompose()`** function in R splits a time series into its components automatically. Always plot the result.
- To choose between additive/multiplicative: **look at the residuals** — they should look like random noise (no pattern, constant variance).
- **Seasonal adjustment** removes $S_t$ so you can see the trend and noise more clearly. For additive: subtract $S_t$. For multiplicative: divide by $S_t$.
- A **Moving Average of order m** smooths the series. The larger $m$ is, the smoother the result. Using $m =$ periodicity removes seasonality entirely.
- There will always be **missing values** at the edges of a moving average: $\frac{m-1}{2}$ at each end for odd $m$, or $\frac{\text{frequency}}{2}$ for seasonal MA.
- Multiple R packages (`forecast`, `TTR`, `zoo`, `slider`) can calculate moving averages and should give the same core result.

---

*This document was created as a study guide for Lab 2. Run all code chunks in RStudio for interactive exploration.*
